title: Missing zero-address check in transferLocks enables permanent token lock with no recovery path
claim: The transferLocks function accepts an unchecked _receiverAddress parameter and only validates that it differs from the sender. No check prevents transfer to address(0). If a lock is transferred to the zero address, the tokens become permanently frozen because: (1) no EOA or contract can have msg.sender == address(0) to satisfy withdrawal authorization checks; (2) the admin recovery function recoverAssets explicitly requires user != address(0), blocking any rescue attempt. This creates an irreversible fund loss scenario with no administrative override.
impact: Any user or buggy integrator contract that passes address(0) as the receiver in transferLocks will permanently lose access to the locked tokens. The protocol owner cannot recover these funds because recoverAssets rejects address(0) as the source user. While this requires an explicit (likely accidental) call with address(0), the lack of a basic safety check combined with the impossibility of recovery elevates this beyond typical user error into a protocol-level missing guardrail.

paths.trigger: A lock owner (withdrawal address) calls transferLocks(depositId, 0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000000) on a lock that has no minted NFT, or on a lock with a minted NFT where nftMinted is false.
paths.exploit: Step 1: Alice has a lock (deposit ID 5) without a minted NFT, with withdrawalAddress = Alice. Step 2: Alice (or a compromised private key, or a buggy smart contract integrating LockToken) calls transferLocks(5, address(0)). Step 3: The function validates msg_sender != address(0) (true, Alice != 0) so it proceeds. Step 4: walletTokenBalance[token][Alice] is decremented, walletTokenBalance[token][address(0)] is incremented. Step 5: lockedToken[5].withdrawalAddress is set to address(0). Step 6: The deposit ID 5 is pushed to depositsByWithdrawalAddress[address(0)]. Step 7: Now no one can withdraw because withdrawTokens requires _msgSender() == lockedERC20.withdrawalAddress (i.e., msg.sender == address(0), which is impossible). Step 8: Owner cannot recover because recoverAssets requires user != address(0). Result: tokens permanently locked.
paths.state_after: The lock's withdrawalAddress is address(0). walletTokenBalance[token][address(0)] holds the balance. No one can ever satisfy the withdrawal authorization check. recoverAssets rejects address(0) as input user, preventing administrative recovery.

paths.trigger: The LockNFT contract calls transferLocks during a safeTransferFrom (3-parameter), but the ERC721 standard safeTransferFrom already rejects to == address(0), so this path is not exploitable through NFT transfers.
paths.exploit: Not exploitable via NFT path because ERC721Upgradeable.safeTransferFrom reverts on to == address(0) before transferLocks would complete, causing full transaction revert.
